rationale_for_baseline_tier: A systemically toxic (fever 39.2, rigors, tachycardia ~118), rapidly spreading facial soft-tissue infection advancing toward the orbit. Telehealth cannot examine the eye (extraocular movements, proptosis, acuity), cannot palpate for necrotising signs, and cannot provide the antibiotics this severity needs — which for a facial, systemically toxic infection is often intravenous. The correct disposition is urgent same-day in-person / emergency-department assessment (T4). The patient is currently haemodynamically stable (BP 115/70, SpO2 97%) without airway compromise, so a baseline of continuous 000/T5 is not required — but 000 is mandated immediately on any orbital, neurological or septic-shock red flag.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness reaching the eyelid/eye, eye pain on movement, double or reduced vision, or the eye bulging (proptosis)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately — do not wait for the arranged in-person appointment
escalation_edges.rationale: New orbital involvement signals orbital cellulitis / cavernous sinus thrombosis — a sight- and life-threatening emergency requiring immediate hospital care and imaging.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness reaches your eyelid or eye, if it hurts to move your eyes, if your vision blurs or doubles, or if an eye starts to bulge — call 000 straight away, do not wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Confusion or drowsiness, feeling faint, a racing heart with clamminess, or the fever climbing despite paracetamol
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate progression toward sepsis / septic shock from a systemically toxic skin infection and need emergency resuscitation and intravenous antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become confused or very drowsy, feel faint, your heart is racing with cold sweats, or the fever keeps rising — call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe pain out of proportion to the appearance, skin turning dusky/black or blistering, or a crackling feel under the skin
escalation_edges.rationale: These are warning signs of necrotising fasciitis — a surgical emergency that cannot be excluded remotely and carries high mortality without immediate operative care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes far worse than the skin looks, the skin darkens or blisters, or it feels like it is crackling under the surface — call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to reach in-person assessment promptly, or the redness visibly advancing over the next hour
escalation_edges.rationale: A rapidly advancing systemically toxic facial infection that cannot be seen in person quickly should default to emergency-department attendance via ambulance rather than waiting.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't be seen in person quickly, or the redness is visibly spreading within the hour, call 000 or go straight to the emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse is present and made the call
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Enlist the spouse to arrange urgent transport, to watch overnight for the orbital/sepsis red flags, and to call 000 on the patient's behalf if he deteriorates or cannot keep fluids or medication down.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Facial location near the orbit with systemic toxicity — telehealth cannot exclude orbital or necrotising extension
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not down-tier on the reassuring patient-reported eye screen; the objective orbital exam is impossible remotely, so urgent in-person assessment stands and 000 applies at the first orbital, neurological or septic sign.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be assessed in person urgently today — go to an emergency department now, or call 000 for an ambulance if you can't get there quickly or if you feel worse. This is a fast-spreading facial infection with a high fever and it can need hospital antibiotics through a drip. In the meantime you can take paracetamol for the fever and pain, keep sipping fluids, and mark the edge of the redness with a pen so any spread is obvious. A telehealth doctor cannot examine your eye or start the antibiotics, which is why you must be seen in person.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Emergency department today; call 000 for an ambulance if unable to attend promptly or if any red flag appears
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This must not wait for clinic hours — the emergency department and 000 are available 24 hours a day and are the correct route tonight.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Take a note of the timeline (sudden fever and rigors then a rapidly spreading raised red facial plaque over ~8 hours), the home temperature (39.2) and wearable heart rate (~118). The hospital will provide a discharge summary; arrange GP review within 24 hours of starting treatment to confirm the border has arrested and the fever is settling.
